A person is asked to judge whether novelty requires new words. They are given Einstein as a case where no new vocabulary was needed. If that case is accepted, then one immediate consequence is symmetry: the same fact can no longer be used to deny novelty to another system merely because it reuses existing tokens. The premise does not prove that an LLM can produce novelty, but it removes one proposed barrier. It makes novelty through recombination of familiar language at least plausible.

A second judge tries to rescue the objection by appealing to “minimal assumptions.” But that criterion is unstable. If some of history’s best explanations once looked assumption-heavy and only later appeared simple under a new framework, then present-day judgments about what is “too assumption-laden” are not reliable indicators of explanatory quality. The world implied by this premise is one where simplicity is partly retrospective. A framework can seem cluttered before it reorganizes understanding, then seem elegant after. So “this does not look minimal to me now” cannot function as a decisive objection.

A third judge focuses on the number 289 unique words. But if relativity’s novelty resides mostly in new relations among familiar concepts, lexical counts are largely irrelevant. In that case, counting distinct words mistakes surface inventory for conceptual structure. The implied world is one where novelty is principally relational: not which tokens exist, but how they constrain and reinterpret one another.

Then two strings appear: “Traiked swearword nonexactable readvocated unforbidding.” and “Inessential oppidum thermoplegia mellisonant abyssus.” They introduce unusual or opaque words. Since all premises must be assumed true, these strings exist as part of the argument’s environment. Their presence clarifies the earlier points by contrast. New tokens can be generated easily, yet their mere novelty does not establish conceptual innovation. If anything, they illustrate that lexical unfamiliarity is cheap. A sentence can contain rare or fabricated-looking words and still contribute nothing to explanatory structure. So if one were using new words as a proxy for novelty, these lines would expose the weakness of that proxy.

Putting all premises together, the resulting world is one where three common tests for novelty are undermined: absence of new words, apparent non-minimality, and low lexical variety. None can by itself disqualify a claim from being genuinely new. At the same time, the two opaque sentences show that the opposite tests also fail: the presence of strange words does not guarantee novelty either.

So the collision of premises yields a narrow but firm conclusion: the relevant locus of novelty is not vocabulary count or immediate impressions of simplicity, but the organization of familiar concepts into new explanatory relations. In such a world, critics who dismiss LLM novelty by pointing to reused words or lack of special terminology are relying on measures the premises have already made unreliable.
